examples.title: Generic PDA Transition Simulator
examples.description: Pseudocode showing how a PDA processes input using its transition function
examples.code:
// PDA Configuration: (state, remaining_input, stack)
// Initialize
current_state = q₀
remaining_input = w // input string
stack = [Z₀] // Z₀ is bottom, stack[0] is top

// Process input
WHILE remaining_input ≠ ε DO
 top = stack.pop()
 IF δ(current_state, remaining_input[0], top) contains (new_state, γ) THEN
 current_state = new_state
 // Push γ in reverse order (rightmost becomes top)
 FOR i FROM length(γ)-1 DOWNTO 0 DO
 stack.push(γ[i])
 END FOR
 remaining_input = remaining_input[1:] // consume one symbol
 ELSE
 REJECT
 END IF
END WHILE

// Acceptance check
IF current_state ∈ F THEN ACCEPT ELSE REJECT
examples.explanation: This pseudocode shows how a PDA processes input step by step. At each step, it looks at the current state, input symbol, and top of stack to determine the next move. The stack is modified by pushing the replacement string in reverse order so the leftmost character ends up on top.
examples.timeComplexity: O(n × k) where n = input length, k = stack operations
examples.spaceComplexity: O(n) for stack in worst case

examples.title: Empty Stack Acceptance PDA for aⁿbⁿ
examples.description: PDA that accepts {aⁿbⁿ | n≥1} by empty stack
examples.code:
// PDA for {aⁿbⁿ | n≥1} by empty stack
// M = (Q, Σ, Γ, δ, q₀, Z₀, F)
// Q = {q₀, q₁}, Σ = {a, b}, Γ = {a, Z₀}, F = {} (empty, using empty stack acceptance)

FUNCTION transition(state, input_symbol, stack_top):
 IF state = q₀ AND input_symbol = 'a' AND stack_top = 'Z₀' THEN
 RETURN [(q₀, 'aZ₀')] // Push 'a'
 ELSE IF state = q₀ AND input_symbol = 'a' AND stack_top = 'a' THEN
 RETURN [(q₀, 'aa')] // Keep pushing 'a's
 ELSE IF state = q₀ AND input_symbol = 'b' AND stack_top = 'a' THEN
 RETURN [(q₁, '')] // Start popping (transition to q₁)
 ELSE IF state = q₁ AND input_symbol = 'b' AND stack_top = 'a' THEN
 RETURN [(q₁, '')] // Keep popping
 ELSE IF state = q₁ AND stack_top = 'Z₀' THEN
 RETURN [(q₁, '')] // Pop Z₀, accept (empty stack)
 ELSE
 RETURN [] // No move - reject
 END IF

// Acceptance: After consuming all input, if stack is empty → ACCEPT
examples.explanation: This PDA works in two phases: 1) While reading 'a's, it pushes each 'a' onto the stack. 2) When first 'b' is read, it transitions to q₁ and starts popping one 'a' for each 'b'. If the input ends exactly when the stack becomes empty (Z₀ is popped), the string is accepted.

examples.title: NPDA Simulation - Non-deterministic Choices
examples.description: How non-determinism works in PDA simulation
examples.code:
// Simulating Non-deterministic PDA
// When δ returns multiple possible moves, try ALL paths

FUNCTION simulate_NPDA(input_string):
 initial_config = (q₀, input_string, [Z₀])
 frontier = [initial_config] // All configurations to explore
 visited = SET() // To avoid infinite loops
 
 WHILE frontier is not empty:
 // Non-deterministically pick a configuration
 config = frontier.pop()
 
 // Skip if already explored similar state
 IF config IN visited: CONTINUE
 ADD config TO visited
 
 // Get possible next moves
 state, remaining, stack = config
 IF remaining = ε: top_symbol = stack[0] else: top_symbol = stack[0]
 input_symbol = remaining[0] if remaining ≠ ε else ε
 
 moves = δ(state, input_symbol, top_symbol)
 
 FOR each (new_state, push_string) IN moves:
 new_config = compute_next_config(state, input_symbol, 
 top_symbol, new_state, push_string)
 
 // If this leads to acceptance, return ACCEPT
 IF is_accepting_config(new_config): RETURN ACCEPT
 
 ADD new_config TO frontier
 END FOR
 END WHILE
 
 RETURN REJECT // No path led to acceptance

FUNCTION is_accepting_config(config):
 state, remaining, stack = config
 // Empty stack acceptance
 IF remaining = ε AND stack = []: RETURN TRUE
 // Final state acceptance 
 IF remaining = ε AND state IN F: RETURN TRUE
 RETURN FALSE
examples.explanation: This simulates non-determinism by exploring all possible paths. If ANY path reaches an accepting configuration (empty stack or final state with consumed input), the entire input is accepted. This brute-force exploration is theoretically important but practically inefficient.
examples.timeComplexity: Exponential in worst case due to non-determinism
examples.spaceComplexity: O(n²) for frontier and visited storage
